The surgeon begins the surgical dissection at the left pelvic sidewall, releasing the congenital attachment of the sigmoid colon at the level of the psoas muscle.
The surgeon prepares to use the ultrasonic scalpel, known as the harmonic device, as the primary instrument for the surgical procedure.

The surgeon addresses advanced congenital adhesions of the sigmoid colon, which extend over adnexal structures and may reach the anterior abdominal wall. The adhesions are being carefully dissected to allow the sigmoid colon to become more retractile.
The surgeon drops down the congenital adhesions of the sigmoid colon, revealing the left adnexa for clear exposure.
Now in any case, whether it is endometriosis or an oncosurgery, the principle of surgery is to create wide exposures, especially from virgin areas.
The psoas muscle is visible above the harmonic scalpel as the surgeon continues dissection to achieve adequate exposure of the muscle.

The surgeon uses small movements of the harmonic scalpel to progressively increase the exposure of the surgical field. The white line is visible and can be followed cranially upwards for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a bowel holding grasper to reveal the fully exposed psoas muscle, which is visible above the instrument.
The surgeon opens the round ligament as laterally as possible, identifying the junction with the obliterated umbilical artery visible above, and divides the round ligament at this level.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical vessel just behind the divided end of the round ligament, using it as a landmark to navigate the lateral side wall.
The surgeon excises the peritoneum parallel to the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing the psoas muscle and its tendon. The external iliac artery becomes visible on the left side of the screen.

The surgeon performs a parallel incision at a greater depth along the infundibulopelvic ligament, opening up the pelvic spaces. The ureter is visible to the right of the harmonic scalpel, while the obliterated umbilical vessel is seen to the left. Behind these structures, the uterine artery is observed crossing over the ureter.
The surgeon holds the obliterated umbilical vessel and tugs on the superior vesical artery, causing visible movement of the bladder as the artery supplies it.

The surgeon holds the obliterated umbilical vessel to develop the medial paravesical space, then shifts focus to the lateral paravesical fossa. The dissection progresses deeply towards the levator floor, aiming to reach the ileococcygeus muscle group.
Both robotic arms are used to perform small dissection movements, reaching the depth of the lateral pelvis. The surgeon proceeds to open the broad ligament, revealing deeper pelvic structures.

The surgeon holds the infundibulopelvic ligament with the left hand while the assistant uses a grasper to display the ureter. The infundibulopelvic ligament is coagulated and divided, creating a wide exposure. The surgeon then opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, extending the incision into the pararectal fossa.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device, resembling a sharp pencil, to develop the medial pararectal space, which is located medial to the ureter. The pararectal space is carefully demarcated and merged with the rectovaginal space at the midline.

The surgeon identifies the hypogastric nerve using Babcock's forceps, applying gentle traction with the harmonic device to delineate the nerve. The nerve is located approximately two centimeters deep from the ureteric level and is preferentially positioned towards the mesorectum, with a visible preference towards the bowel side.

The surgeon develops the iliac tree and lateral pararectal fossa, revealing the uterine artery coursing medially upwards. Dissection continues below the uterine artery, exposing the deep uterine vein.

The surgeon uses a harmonic blade to dissect around the ureter, which is positioned between the uterine artery and the deep uterine vein. The ureter is clearly visible as the surgeon holds the uterine artery with the left hand and divides its branch to the ureter, facilitating easier dissection of the ureter.

The surgeon holds the ureter with the left hand and deperitonizes it by removing its attachment to the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. This process involves carefully detaching the peritoneum from the ureter.

The assistant uses a grasper to retrovert the uterus while the surgeon pushes down the bladder, exposing the medial pillar, also known as the anterior parametra or vesico-uterine ligament. The surgeon prepares to dissect the bladder and develop the uterovesical and vesicovaginal spaces, crucial for completing the ureteric dissection to the bladder.

The surgeon dissects the medial pillar, also known as the anterior parametra, to facilitate the unroofing of the ureter. The uterine artery, previously preserved, is now coagulated and divided. As the dissection progresses deeper, the vaginal artery is identified traveling towards the vagina.

The surgeon identifies the deep uterine vein, which is positioned below the uterine artery and ureter, and is postulated by some authorities to be the vaginal vein. The superficial vein traveling with the uterine artery is identified as the uterine vein proper. The nerves are located deep to the deep uterine vein.

The surgeon opens the pelvic triangle window on the right side, revealing the external iliac vein as a blue structure. As the dissection progresses upwards, the obliterated umbilical vessel becomes visible, serving as a landmark for delineating the iliac tree.

As the surgical space expands, the branches of the iliac tree become visible, including the uterine artery and the superior vesicle artery. The surgeon tugs on the superior vesicle artery, demonstrating its movement towards the bladder. The uterine artery is seen traversing like a transverse bridge. Deeper exploration reveals the vaginal vessels, and further dissection towards the ischial spine exposes the inferior vesicle artery. The ureter is visible on the left side, adhered to the peritoneal leaf.

The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the ureter traveling beneath the uterine artery. The deep uterine vein is visible, draining into the internal iliac vein. The white structure identified as the vaginal artery is seen traveling towards the paracervical tissue.
The assistant uses a grasper to lift the uterine artery while the surgeon dissects along the superior surface of the ureter, further lateralizing it and tracing its path towards the bladder.

The surgeon performs diuretic dissection, revealing the deep uterine vein positioned below the ureter. The ureter is held by a Babcock clamp and then released, while the uterine artery remains undivided, tented above the ureter. The vesicle vein is visible draining into the deep uterine vein, with the surgeon using a harmonic device to gently tug the vertical blue vein.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine artery from its origin to the internal os, observing its upward turn as it becomes the ascending uterine artery. The medial pillar is divided, completing the tunnel dissection. The ureter is seen rolling into the bladder, with the uterine artery fully separated and intact.

The surgeon identifies the uterine artery and the hypogastric nerve, along with the vaginal vessels, including the deep uterine vein and vaginal artery. The lateral parametra, consisting of the uterine artery and its fascia, is dissected towards the uterine side, enhancing the visibility of the anterior parametra. The uterine artery is noted for its toughness, elasticity, coiled structure, and length.

The assistant grasper holds the posterior utero-sacral ligament while the surgeon completes the hysterectomy. The harmonic device cuts into the peritoneum, and the rectovaginal space is developed, exposing the two layers of Denonvilliers' fascia. The surgeon delineates the utero-sacral ligament, also known as the sacro-uterine ligament, and identifies the rectovaginal ligament, which attaches the rectum to the vagina. The rectal serosa is visible as the posterior parametra is held and cut.
